summary: High-risk regulatory changes significantly impact food seasoning/snack compliance. Key updates include: **United States** added sesame as a major allergen (effective Jan 1, 2023) with new detailed labeling requirements for all major allergens, clarified gluten-free claims, set new contaminant limits (3-MCPD, Aflatoxins), and prohibited/mandated declaration for specific additives/colors. **Singapore** introduced stricter rules for food additives (SFA assessment, permitted list, prohibition of unpermitted) and contaminants (SFA limits, prohibition of exceeding, specific drug residue bans), alongside mandatory ingredient/additive labeling. The **GCC** has new high-risk requirements for allergen declarations, general labeling, Halal food, mandatory expiration dates, and microbiological limits. **India** now requires specified allergen declarations. **Japan** introduced new general contaminant limits and microbiological standards. **Taiwan** expanded its mandatory allergen list, including sulphite (≥10 mg/kg). **Thailand** requires verification of permitted/non-permitted ingredients and has a new list of prohibited foods. **Codex** updated guidance for contaminants and naturally occurring toxicants. Moderate risk changes involve various updates to additive, contaminant, and labeling standards across China, EU, India, Indonesia, and Japan, with several older rules removed, requiring source verification.

changes.oldLimit: Verification required via Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and CCCF established levels
changes.newLimit: Verification required via the Codex General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) and levels established by the Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF)
changes.notes: Codex establishes Maximum Levels (MLs) for contaminants to be legally permitted in food or feed commodities to protect human health. The CCCF establishes and endorses these levels.

This technical regulation for prepackaged food labels is expected to include requirements for allergen declarations. Specifics on the list of mandatory allergens and declaration format require consulting the full text of the regulation.
Specific labelling requirements for prepackaged food stuffs must be verified by consulting the full GSO 9:2022 standard.
Specific general requirements for Halal food must be verified by consulting the full GSO 2055-1:2015 standard.
Specific mandatory expiration date requirements for food products must be verified by consulting the full GSO 150-1:2013 standard.
Specific microbiological limits for foodstuffs and food ingredients must be verified by consulting the full GSO 1016:2015 standard.
All food products, including seasoning and snack products, must declare specified allergens as per FSSAI Labelling Regulations.

Compliance with the 'Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act' is required, which includes standards for contaminants in food. Verification of specific limits is needed.

Compliance with the 'Specifications and Standards for Food, Food Additives, etc. Under The Food Sanitation Act' is required, which includes microbiological standards for food. Verification of specific limits is needed.

changes.newLimit: Must be assessed for safety by SFA
changes.notes: Applies to all food sold in Singapore.

changes.oldLimit: Verification required using SFA's List of Permitted Food Additives or Food Additives Search tool
changes.notes: Verification required using SFA's List of Permitted Food Additives or Food Additives Search tool.

changes.newLimit: Do not import or sell food products containing unpermitted additives
changes.notes: An application can be submitted to SFA to evaluate the use of an additive.

changes.oldLimit: Do not import or sell food products containing contaminants exceeding regulatory limits
changes.newLimit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits
Do not import or sell food products containing contaminants exceeding regulatory limits

changes.notes: Prohibition under Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations.

changes.newLimit: All ingredients and additives must be declared on food labels
changes.notes: Applies to prepacked foods for sale in Singapore.
Specific use restrictions and labeling requirements for γ-Aminobutyric Acid (GABA) produced by microbial fermentation must be verified.

Food allergen labeling is required for products containing Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish, and sulphite (as residue sulphur dioxide for 10 or more mg/kg of food) as an ingredient or food additive.
If sulphite as residue sulphur dioxide is 10 or more milligrams per kilogram of food, mandatory allergen labeling is required.
All ingredients used in food products must be verified against the list of permitted and non-permitted ingredients.
Verification is required to ensure the food product is not on the list of prohibited foods for production, import, or sale.
Delisted color additives in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30).
Substances listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) as prohibited from use in food.

changes.newLimit: Milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, sesame
changes.notes: The FASTER Act declared sesame as the 9th major food allergen, effective January 1, 2023.
This is a Guidance Level for Acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces.
This is an Action Level for 'Foods' generally.
Must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.
All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 must identify it on their labels.
A final rule defines “gluten-free” for food labeling. Compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the “gluten-free” claim were established on August 12, 2020.
The name of the food source of a major food allergen must appear immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement.
The name of the food source of a major food allergen must appear in parentheses following the name of the ingredient.

changes.parameter: Specific Allergen Declaration (Tree Nuts, Fish, Shellfish)
changes.notes: The type of tree nut, and the species of fish and Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
Must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
Compliance with GB2760-2015 is mandatory for the use of food additives in China.

changes.notes: The GSFA (Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth conditions for permitted food additives in all foods. The database is regularly updated and users are encouraged to consult it and its Preamble.

changes.newLimit: Follow the Code of Practice for the Prevention and Reduction of Mycotoxins in Spices
changes.notes: This is a specific code of practice (CXC 78-2017) for reducing mycotoxin contamination in spices.
The EU Additives Database provides information on approved food additives and their conditions of use, based on Annex II of Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008.

This standard provides guidelines for optional shelf life periods for food products. If an optional shelf life is declared, it must comply with the standard. Specifics require consulting the full text of the standard.

changes.notes: Suppliers may have test information, or samples can be sent to SFA-recognized laboratories.

changes.newLimit: Not required to be tested for TPC

changes.notes: Food allergen labeling is recommended for products containing Cephalopods and cephalopod products, Spiral shells and Spiral shell products, Seeds and seed products (excluding highly refined or purified sunflower seed oil), and Kiwis and kiwi products as an ingredient or food additive.

A precautionary allergen labeling statement is suggested if there is a risk of allergen cross-contamination, such as 'The plant, equipment or production line used to produce the product is also used to process○○' or synonymous terms.
